1: "Lot was sitting in the gateway of the city (yōšēḇ bĕšaʿar-Sĕdōm)" — the city gate was the civic center of ANE cities: judicial proceedings (Deuteronomy 21:19; Ruth 4:1-11), commercial transactions (2 Kings 7:1), and civic administration (Proverbs 31:23; Job 29:7-10) all took place at the gate. Lot's position in the gateway in 19:1 marks his full civic integration into Sodom — contrasted with his earlier progressions: "near Sodom" (Genesis 13:12), "among the cities of the plain" (Genesis 13:12), "in Sodom" (Genesis 14:12), and now "in the gateway." He is a civic leader, not merely a resident; 2 Peter 2:7-8 calls him "righteous Lot" who was "distressed by the depraved conduct of the lawless" — a righteous man at the center of an unrighteous city.
2: Lot's urgent invitation — "please turn aside to your servant's house" — mirrors Abraham's hospitality of Genesis 18, using the same vocabulary of "servant," foot-washing, and overnight stay. But where Abraham pressed from genuine hospitality abundance, Lot's urgency has a protective edge: he "insisted strongly (wayyiṣpôr bām mĕʾōḏ)" because he knew what awaited unprotected strangers in Sodom's city square after dark. The visitors' initial refusal — "we will spend the night in the square" — tests Lot's commitment; his persistence is the act that saves the visitors from the mob.
3: "Baking bread without yeast (maṣṣôt)" — unleavened bread, the same term for the Passover bread (Exodus 12:8,17-20,39). The narrative echo may be intentional: Lot's "Passover-like" meal on the night before the destruction parallels Israel's Passover meal on the night before the Exodus. Both narratives involve a rescue through divine action, a night of danger and judgment, and a commanded departure at dawn. The rapid preparation of unleavened bread (no waiting for yeast to rise) is the bread of urgency — consistent with the night's narrative tempo.
4: "All the men from every part of the city of Sodom — both young and old — surrounded the house" — the comprehensive participation in the demand is the narrative's most damning detail. Not a criminal fringe but "all the men" (kol-hāʿam miqqāṣeh, all the people from every end) of the city; not youthful indiscretion but "both young and old" (minnāʿar wĕʿad-zāqēn), excluding the "ignorance of youth" or "decay of age" explanations. The comprehensiveness establishes that the corruption of Sodom is total — which is why the intercession of Genesis 18 ultimately finds no adequate righteous remnant.
5: The demand — "bring them out to us so that we can have sex with them (wĕnēḏĕʿāh ʾōtām)" — the verb yādaʿ (to know) in a sexual context, the same used in Genesis 4:1 (Adam "knew" Eve) and Genesis 24:16 (Rebekah "not known" by a man). The sexual violence intended against the visitors is the act that gives the name "sodomy" to its primary historical association. The broader sin-picture of Sodom in Ezekiel 16:49-50 ("arrogance, overfed and unconcerned; they did not help the poor and needy, and they did detestable things before me") contextualizes Genesis 19's specific scene within a pattern of comprehensive covenant-violation: the sexual violence is the culmination, not the totality, of Sodom's sin.
6: "Lot went outside to meet them and shut the door behind him" — the physical act of interposing himself between the visitors and the mob is the cost of the ANE hospitality-obligation. A host who took a guest "under his roof" (taḥat ẓēl qōrātî, v8: under the shelter of my roof-beam) was honor-bound to protect that guest at personal cost. Lot's shutting of the door (protecting the visitors inside) while he goes out alone to face the mob is the maximum personal risk the hospitality obligation required. His willingness to face the mob alone is the act that 2 Peter 2:8 credits to his "righteous soul tormented day after day by lawless deeds."
7: "Do not do this wicked thing (rāʿāh)" — the same word used by the narrator in 13:13 for Sodom's wickedness. Lot's moral objection to the mob's demand uses the narrator's own evaluative vocabulary — he sees the evil correctly, even if his proposed solution (v8) is itself deeply problematic. The moral clarity of Lot's objection ("this is wicked") stands against the total moral numbness of the mob. The contrast establishes Lot's "righteous" character (2 Peter 2:8) within Sodom's context — he is relatively righteous compared to the city, not absolutely righteous by covenant standards.
8: Lot's offer of his daughters — the most difficult passage in Genesis 19. The ANE honor-context: a guest "under the roof" was under the absolute protection of the host; the host's honor was entirely bound to the guest's safety. Lot's offer of his daughters in place of the guests reflects this extreme priority: protecting the guests from dishonor by offering the host's own family instead. The text does not commend this action — the daughters are also under Lot's protection — but the narrative presents it as the moment of maximum desperation within the host-honor framework. The lot of Lot's daughters (violence offered by their own father to protect strangers) is the tragic dimension of living within a city whose violence has normalized the impossible choice.
9: "This fellow came here as a foreigner (gēr bāʾ)" — the mob weaponizes Lot's outsider status against his moral objection. He is a gēr (resident alien) in Sodom — accepted but not fully belonging, without full civic standing to "play the judge (šāpōṭ yišpôṭ)" over the city. The irony is double: Lot is a gēr in Sodom (where he has no land rights despite civic presence) just as Abraham is a gēr in Canaan (Genesis 17:8). The mob's "who gave you authority to judge us?" echoes the Israelite worker's challenge to Moses (Exodus 2:14: "who made you ruler and judge over us?") — the pattern of rejecting the covenant-person's moral authority.
10: "But the men inside reached out and pulled Lot back into the house and shut the door" — the angelic intervention is the turning point of the narrative. The angels act here with physical force: reaching out, pulling, shutting — not supernatural manifestation but direct physical action. Their protection of Lot reverses his protective gesture of v6: he went out to protect them; they pull him in to protect him. The narrative dynamic models the pattern of divine rescue: the covenant-person risks to protect the divine visitor; the divine visitor ultimately rescues the covenant-person.
11: "They struck the men... with blindness (sanewerîm)" — the word sanewerîm is a rare term used only here and in 2 Kings 6:18 (the Aramean army struck blind at Elisha's request at Dothan). Its etymology is uncertain — possibly related to a Mesopotamian word for sudden blindness or disorientation. "So that they could not find the door" — the mob, though blinded, does not disperse but continues seeking the door; their persistence in blindness (literally wearing themselves out seeking an entrance they cannot find) is the narrative's most disturbing detail: the compulsive violence continues even when it cannot succeed.
12: "Do you have anyone else here — sons-in-law, sons or daughters, or anyone else in the city who belongs to you?" — the angels' rescue mandate is comprehensive: they are sent to save all who "belong to" Lot (covenant household) before destruction. The question reveals the rescue's scope: not just Lot individually but his entire household network. The "sons-in-law, sons or daughters" indicates Lot has daughters beyond the two at home (the "daughters pledged to marry," v14, are additional — the two daughters who escape are described as "who are here" suggesting they are the unmarried ones still in the household).
13: "We are going to destroy this place" — the angels' self-disclosure as agents of divine judgment. "The outcry to the LORD against its people is so great that he has sent us to destroy it (šālaḥ YHWH ʾōtānû lĕšaḥătāh)" — the divine-mission language echoes prophetic commission vocabulary; the angels are sent agents executing a predetermined judgment. The disclosure to Lot of what is coming — before it happens — follows the same pattern as YHWH's disclosure to Abraham in Genesis 18:17: covenant partners are given foreknowledge of divine action that affects them.
14: "His sons-in-law thought he was joking (wayhî kĕmĕṣaḥēq bĕʿênê ḥătānāyw)" — the verb ṣāḥaq (to laugh/play/jest) is the same root as Isaac's name (yiṣḥāq). The ironic intersection: in the Abraham narrative, the laughter-root produces the child of covenant promise; in the Lot narrative, the same root produces the comedy of fatal disbelief — those who laughed off the warning perished. The sons-in-law's treatment of the warning as "joking" is the practical effect of living in Sodom's moral atmosphere: a city that mocks judgment cannot hear the warning of judgment seriously.
15: "With the coming of dawn, the angels urged (wayyāʾîṣû) Lot" — the urgency of the escape coincides with the dawn; the destruction is coming with the daylight (v23-24). The dawn urgency echoes the Passover departure at night, completed before morning (Exodus 12:22: "none of you shall go out the door until morning"). "Or you will be swept away (tissāpeh) when the city is punished (baʿăwôn hāʿîr)" — the same word sāpāh (sweep away) used by Abraham in his intercession (Genesis 18:23: "will you sweep away the righteous with the wicked?"). The angels are fulfilling the judgment that Abraham's intercession tried to avert.
16: "When he hesitated (wayyitmahĕhāh)" — Lot's hesitation at the moment of rescue is the narrative's most piercing detail. The verb māhah (to delay/linger) appears only here and in Judges 3:26 (context of escape) and Genesis 43:10 (if we had not delayed). Even facing imminent destruction, Lot cannot bring himself to leave the city he has made his home. "The men grasped his hand and the hands of his wife and of his two daughters and led them safely out of the city, for the LORD was merciful (bĕḥemlat YHWH ʿālāyw) to them" — divine mercy operates through physical compulsion: the covenant-person who cannot rescue himself is physically removed by divine agents. The mercy is the reason for the rescue; the hesitation is the reason the rescue required force.
17: "Flee for your lives! Don't look back (ʾal-tabbiṭ ʾaḥărêkā)!" — the prohibition against looking back will be violated by Lot's wife in v26. The prohibition has been interpreted as: a practical command (looking back slows escape); a covenant-boundary instruction (the condemned territory must be left completely); or a symbolic command (the past must not be clung to in the moment of rescue). Jesus' citation — "Remember Lot's wife" (Luke 17:32) — uses it as a warning against turning back from the kingdom of God when the moment of decision comes. "Don't stop anywhere in the plain" — the entire Jordan plain is under judgment; partial evacuation is not safe.
18: "But Lot said to them, 'No, my lords, please (ʾal-nāʾ ʾăḏōnāy)!'" — Lot addresses the angels with the same ʾăḏōnāy (my lords/Lord) used by Abraham in Genesis 18:3. The negotiation Lot initiates — asking for an alternative escape destination — is remarkable: mid-rescue, he is negotiating terms. The freedom to negotiate with the divine messenger even in a moment of extreme urgency reflects the ANE covenant-relationship between YHWH and his covenant partners: they are not merely commanded but engaged. The angel's willingness to grant the alternative (v21) confirms this.
19: "I can't flee to the mountains; this disaster will overtake me, and I'll die" — Lot's practical objection to the commanded mountain-escape reflects his assessment of his own capacity: he cannot run far enough or fast enough to reach the mountains before the destruction overtakes him. The mountains (probably Edom/Moab's highland to the southeast) would require a longer journey. Whether the assessment is accurate or whether it reflects Lot's attachment to the settled-city life — he wants to go to a town (v20), not to the wilderness — the narrative presents it without critique. YHWH grants the alternative, suggesting the objection was valid.
20: "Here is a town near enough to run to, and it is small — it is very small (miṣʿār hîʾ), isn't it?" — the smallness of Zoar is doubly emphasized (small... very small), and the name Zoar (ṣōʿar) means "insignificant/small" in Hebrew. The place-name etymology preserves the story: Zoar is named for what Lot said about it in his moment of rescue-negotiation. The preservation of the city's name in Hebrew meaning captures the mercy granted: a small city was spared for a small reason. "Then my life will be spared (tĕḥî napšî)" — literally "my nepeš will live"; the same word used in the Psalms for the soul's survival and deliverance (Psalms 33:19; Genesis 116:8).
21: "Very well, I will grant this request too (šāʾtî ʾet-pānêkā gam liḏḇār hazzeh)" — literally "I have lifted your face" — the ANE idiom for granting a petition and showing favor. The "too" (gam) acknowledges that Lot has already received the major grace of rescue; the alternative destination is an additional concession. "I will not overthrow the town you speak of" — Zoar is explicitly included in the judgment zone (it is one of the five cities, Genesis 14:2) but is spared for Lot's sake. The rescue of a condemned city for the sake of one covenant-person is the most extended expression of the Genesis 18 principle: the righteous save the community.
22: "I cannot do anything until you reach it (kî lōʾ ʾûkal laʿăśôt dāḇār ʿad-bōʾăkā šāmmāh)" — the divine restraint awaiting Lot's safety is one of the most remarkable statements in Genesis: the execution of judgment is held back by the presence of the righteous. The same principle governs 2 Peter 3:9 (the Lord "is patient, not wanting anyone to perish") and the eschatological withholding of judgment in Revelation. "That is why the town was called Zoar (ṣōʿar)" — the name-etymology formalizes the narrative memory in the place-name: the insignificant small city is named for the moment when significance was granted to the insignificant for the sake of one hesitating man.
23: "By the time Lot reached Zoar, the sun had risen over the land" — the timing note establishes the daylight context for the destruction: not a secret act in darkness but a judicial act in full daylight. The sun rising over the land (haššemeš yāṣāʾ ʿal-hāʾāreṣ) marks the transition from the night of rescue to the day of judgment. The same temporal movement — night of salvation / day of judgment — structures the Passover: the destroying angel passes over in the night; Israel departs in the morning; Pharaoh's army is destroyed at the Sea. The Sodom narrative may deliberately anticipate the Exodus pattern.
24: "The LORD rained down burning sulfur (gāprît wāʾēš) on Sodom and Gomorrah — from the LORD out of the heavens (mēʾēt YHWH min-haššāmāyim)" — the double mention of YHWH ("the LORD rained... from the LORD out of the heavens") is a Hebrew construction emphasizing the divine source. Ancient interpreters (Justin Martyr, Tertullian) read two divine persons; modern scholarship reads an ANE idiom for emphatic divine agency: the judgment is entirely YHWH's, mediated through natural forces (volcanic sulfur-fire, geological upheaval, or divine incendiary action). Burning sulfur and fire are the judgment materials of prophetic imagery throughout the OT (Isaiah 34:9; Ezekiel 38:22; Psalms 11:6) and NT (Revelation 19:20; Genesis 20:10; Genesis 21:8).
25: "He overthrew those cities and the entire plain (kol-hakkikār), destroying all those living in the cities — and also the vegetation in the land (ṣĕmaḥ hāʾăḏāmāh)" — the ecological totality of the destruction. The once "well-watered" Jordan plain "like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt" (Genesis 13:10) becomes ash and desolation. The vegetation (ṣĕmaḥ) destruction is significant: the land's fertility — which had made the plain attractive to Lot — is annihilated. The city that consumed rather than produced righteousness loses the ground-fertility that sustained it. Deuteronomy 29:23 will use the Sodom-Gomorrah desolation as the paradigm of covenant-curse: a land of "burning sulfur and salt — nothing planted, nothing sprouting."
26: "But Lot's wife looked back (wattabbēṭ ʾištô mēʾaḥărāyw), and she became a pillar of salt (nĕṣîḇ melaḥ)" — salt-formations in the Dead Sea region (visible still today as pillar-shaped salt columns) provided the geographic memory for this narrative. The violation of the "don't look back" command (v17) results in the destruction she was fleeing claiming her — she is turned into the landscape of the judgment zone she could not leave behind. Jesus' citation — "remember Lot's wife" (Luke 17:32) — uses her fate as a warning about the cost of turning back when God commands forward movement: to look back is to belong to what is being destroyed.
27: "Abraham got up and returned to the place where he had stood before the LORD (ʿāmad lĕpānê YHWH)" — the same "standing before the LORD" of Abraham's intercession in Genesis 18:22. Abraham returns to the site of intercession to observe what became of the city he pleaded for. "The place where he had stood" marks the intercessor's location as a covenant-memory site — where the negotiation with YHWH happened. The morning return is the covenant partner's reckoning with the outcome of his intercession: the prayer was offered; the judgment proceeded; the intercessor witnesses both.
28: "He saw dense smoke rising from the land, like smoke from a furnace (kĕqîṭōr hakkibbšān)" — the furnace-smoke simile uses kibbšān, the same word for the Sinai theophany's smoke: "Mount Sinai was covered in smoke, because the LORD descended on it in fire. The smoke billowed up from it like smoke from a furnace (kĕqîṭōr hakkibbšān)" (Exodus 19:18). The same image for Sodom's destruction and Sinai's theophany creates an ominous intertextual echo: the divine presence in fire and smoke is simultaneously the presence of covenant and the presence of judgment. Abraham witnesses what his intercession could not prevent.
29: "God remembered Abraham (wayyizkōr ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-ʾaḇrāhām)" — the covenant-memory formula (as in Genesis 8:1: "God remembered Noah"). Lot's rescue is explicitly motivated by the Abrahamic covenant: "he brought Lot out of the catastrophe... when God destroyed the cities." Lot is saved not for his own merit alone but because YHWH's covenant with Abraham extends protection to Abraham's kin — the covenant's reach includes the covenant-partner's household network. The formula "God remembered" in Genesis always marks a pivot from crisis to rescue driven by covenant-loyalty, not human initiative (cf. Genesis 30:22: "God remembered Rachel"; Exodus 2:24: "God remembered his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob").
30: "Lot and his two daughters left Zoar and settled in the mountains" — the circuit of Lot's journey: he went from Canaan (covenant land) to the Jordan plain (chosen by sight, Genesis 13:10-11), to near Sodom (Genesis 13:12), to in Sodom (Genesis 14:12), to the gateway of Sodom (Genesis 19:1), to Zoar (rescued but still settled), to a mountain cave (isolated, stationary). Each move is downward in covenant terms: from the pilgrim-tent to the city-gate to the cave. The cave-dwelling (yayyēšeḇ bammĕʿārāh) marks the end of Lot's narrative arc — the contrast with Abraham's tent-dwelling (hospitality, mobility, covenant journey) is complete.
31: "There is no man around here to give us children — as is the custom all over the earth (kĕdereḵ kol-hāʾāreṣ)" — the daughters' reasoning reflects a perceived catastrophe: they believe the entire world has been destroyed. Isolated in a mountain cave, having witnessed the destruction of the Jordan plain's cities, with no male relatives surviving (the sons-in-law had refused to flee), the daughters conclude that human civilization has ended. Their reasoning is not presented as correct but as the perception that drives the action. The phrase "as is the custom all over the earth" implies they believe normal human reproduction must continue — the obligation of survival-reproduction felt as universal.
32: "Let's get our father to drink wine and then sleep with him and preserve our family line (nĕḥayyeh zera, keep alive seed/descendants) through our father" — the plan is presented in terms of the daughters' survival-reasoning, not as a moral endorsement. The same vocabulary of "keeping alive seed" (zera) is used in the Lot-narrative's own preceding context: the rescue from Sodom was to preserve the family (leḥayyōt napeš, Genesis 19:19). The daughters' reasoning extends this survival imperative to its logical limit — the family line must continue even by transgressing the incest prohibition. The text presents the plan without authorial comment; the reader is left to supply the moral evaluation.
33: "He was not aware of it when she lay down or when she got up" — the repeated formula in vv33 and 35 insists on Lot's complete unawareness; his drunkenness is not complicity but the precondition the daughters exploit. The narrative detail protects Lot from intentional incest (which would have been the greater moral violation in ANE law) while not sparing him the consequences: the actions produce children and ultimately nations. The wine-induced unconsciousness and the formulaic repetition of unawareness serve the narrative's interest in explaining the Moabite and Ammonite origins without condemning Lot as a knowing participant.
34: The older daughter's plan for the younger follows the same pattern as v32-33 — the repetition is deliberate: the narrative gives equal weight to both events, establishing the symmetry of the two national origins. The older-then-younger sequence mirrors the reversal pattern in Genesis (Esau/Jacob, Manasseh/Ephraim) where the expected first-born priority is overturned; here the older daughter acts first and bears Moab, the older nation-neighbor to Israel.
35: "Again he was not aware of it" — the formula repeated from v33; the narrative insists on the parallelism. The structural regularity of the two events (same night-wine-sleep pattern, same unawareness, same outcome) is the narrative's way of treating the two national origins with equal dignity and equal qualification: neither Moab nor Ammon has a more honorable or less honorable origin than the other. Both nations are born from the same crisis situation of perceived total isolation.
36: "So both of Lot's daughters became pregnant by their father" — the narrative concludes the account with the bare statement of outcome. The pregnancy is the goal the daughters had intended; the bare statement of achievement is the narrative's minimal comment. The moral evaluation is left entirely to the reader — the text neither condemns nor approves, but the placement of this episode after the rescue of a "righteous" man who hesitated to leave Sodom and whose wife turned to salt creates the portrait of covenant damage: even the righteous person drawn too deeply into a condemned culture emerges impaired.
37: "The older daughter had a son, and she named him Moab (Mōʾāḇ)" — the name Moab may be a wordplay on mēʾāḇ (from father) — explicitly noting the origin of the Moabite nation. The Moabites of the OT are Israel's persistent neighbors-and-enemies to the east of the Jordan: Balak of Moab hires Balaam against Israel (Num 22-24); David subdues Moab (2 Samuel 8:2); Mesha of Moab rebels against Israel (2 Kgs 3; the Mesha Stele). But Ruth the Moabitess is the great-grandmother of David (Ruth 4:13-22) — making a Moabitess (descended from Lot's incestuous union) an ancestor of the covenant-king and ultimately of Christ (Matthew 1:5). The narrative's irony is complete: the excluded-and-disgraced line contributes to the messianic lineage.
38: "The younger daughter also had a son, and she named him Ben-Ammi (ben-ʿammî)" — "son of my people/kinsman" — he is the father of the Ammonites. The Ammonites occupy the territory east of the Jordan north of Moab; their capital Rabbah (modern Amman, Jordan) preserves the name. The Ammonites are among Israel's persistent adversaries (1 Sam 11; 2 Sam 10-12; Amos 1:13-15; Jeremiah 49:1-6). The etiological function of the passage — explaining the origin of two historically significant neighbor-nations — is clear: the Moabites and Ammonites are Lot's descendants, which establishes their relationship to Israel (Semitic cousins, closer than Canaanites or Egyptians) while also marking their origin outside the covenant line.
